target.title: fix(agents): restore global subagent model default priority over agent own model
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #58822.

- Restores correct subagent model precedence in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`: global `agents.defaults.subagents.model` now takes priority over the parent agent's own `model.primary`, with the agent's own model only used as a last-resort fallback
- Applies the same fix to the cron isolated-agent model selection path (`src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`) and the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper in `src/agents/agent-scope.ts`
- Updates existing tests from #58003 to assert the corrected behavior and adds new coverage for main-agent, named-agent, and explicit-override scenarios

### Background

PR #58003 reordered `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection` to prefer `agentConfig.model` over `agents.defaults.subagents.model`. While this was intended to let named agents keep their own model for subagents, it caused a regression: when the main agent runs Opus and `defaults.subagents.model` is GPT-5.4, subagents incorrectly inherited Opus because the agent's own model shadowed the global subagent default.

The correct precedence is:
1. `agentConfig.subagents.model` — explicit per-agent subagent config (highest)
2. `agents.defaults.subagents.model` — global subagent default
3. `agentConfig.model` — agent's own model (last-resort fallback)

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts` — 105 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` — 32 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.model.test.ts` — 11 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts` — 1 test passes
- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` — 84 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts` — 34 tests pass
- [x] Test: main agent (Opus) with global subagent default (GPT-5.4) → subagents use GPT-5.4
- [x] Test: named agent without per-agent subagents.model → uses global default
- [x] Test: falls back to agent own model when no global default set
- [x] Test: explicit model override in spawn always wins

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Subagent model resolution for a main agent whose own `model` differs from `agents.defaults.subagents.model`. Before this PR, an Opus-running agent with `defaults.subagents.model = openai/gpt-5` still produced Opus subagents because the agent's own `model` shadowed the global subagent default in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`, in the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper, and in the cron isolated-agent path. This PR restores the documented precedence so the global default wins over the agent's primary model, while per-agent `subagents.model` still takes the very top slot.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64 (Node v24.15.0), local checkout of `fix/subagent-model-precedence-global-default` at HEAD `81168e5de5`, dependencies installed via `pnpm install`. Resolvers exercised by importing the actual TypeScript source under `src/agents/model-selection.ts` and `src/agents/agent-scope.ts` via the `tsx` loader — no mocks, no Vitest, no provider catalogs swapped out. The cron isolated-agent path uses the same `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper, so the asserted source values cover that resolver too.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ cd pr-58823
$ pnpm install --prefer-offline
$ node --import tsx scripts/proof/pr-58823-subagent-precedence.mjs
```

The script (`scripts/proof/pr-58823-subagent-precedence.mjs`, committed in this PR for reviewer reproducibility) constructs three real `OpenClawConfig` shapes, calls `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult`, `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`, and `resolveSubagentSpawnModelSelection` on each, and asserts both the resolved model ref and the selection `source` label (`subagent` / `default-subagent` / `agent`).

**Evidence after fix:** Verbatim terminal stdout (runtime log) from the live `node --import tsx` run above against the patched sources:

```
PR #58823 subagent model precedence — real-behavior proof
repo head: (local worktree)
node: v24.15.0

--- scenario A — global default beats agent primary ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = openai/gpt-5
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = (unset)
agent-scope source                   = default-subagent
agent-scope raw                      = "openai/gpt-5"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = openai/gpt-5
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = openai/gpt-5
OK scenario A — global default beats agent primary

--- scenario B — per-agent override wins ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = openai/gpt-5
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = openai/gpt-5-mini
agent-scope source                   = subagent
agent-scope raw                      = "openai/gpt-5-mini"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = openai/gpt-5-mini
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = openai/gpt-5-mini
OK scenario B — per-agent override wins

--- scenario C — falls through to agent primary ---
input.agentConfig.model              = anthropic/claude-opus
input.defaults.subagents.model       = (unset)
input.agentConfig.subagents.model    = (unset)
agent-scope source                   = agent
agent-scope raw                      = "anthropic/claude-opus"
model-selection.resolveConfigured()  = anthropic/claude-opus
model-selection.resolveSpawn()       = anthropic/claude-opus
OK scenario C — falls through to agent primary

PROOF PASSED — subagent model precedence follows: per-agent > defaults.subagents > agent primary
```

For completeness, the Vitest suites listed in Test plan also pass on this branch (`pnpm test src/agents/model-selection.test.ts` → 105 passed; `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` → 32 passed; etc.), but the authoritative proof above is the live `node --import tsx` run against the actual patched source files, not the unit suite.

**Observed result after fix:**

- Scenario A (Opus main agent, `defaults.subagents.model = openai/gpt-5`, no per-agent override): the live resolver returns `openai/gpt-5` with `source = default-subagent`. Before this PR it returned `anthropic/claude-opus` with `source = agent` — the exact bug from #58822.
- Scenario B (per-agent `subagents.model = openai/gpt-5-mini` set alongside the global default): `openai/gpt-5-mini` with `source = subagent` wins. Per-agent override still beats the global default, confirming the highest-priority rung is intact.
- Scenario C (neither per-agent `subagents.model` nor `defaults.subagents.model` set): resolves to the agent's own `model` (`anthropic/claude-opus`) with `source = agent`, confirming the documented last-resort fallback is preserved.

The `node` exit code was `0` (proof script asserts each expectation; first mismatch would throw). The `agent-scope` source labels are produced by the shared `resolveSubagentModelConfigSelectionResult` helper used by both `src/agents/model-selection.ts` and `src/cron/isolated-agent/model-selection.ts`, so a passing scope source label means the cron path inherits the same precedence.

**What was not tested:** Cross-OS runtime (macOS / Windows) — code path is platform-agnostic config resolution, no filesystem or shell dependencies. Live spawn of an actual subagent process against a remote model provider — the resolver returns the same model ref regardless of whether the downstream spawn succeeds, and the spawn path is exercised by `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.model.test.ts` (11 passing). UI surfacing of the resolved subagent model in the gateway is also unchanged by this PR and not re-tested here.

target.title: Fix ACP image forwarding and Synology invalid-token throttling
target.body:
## Summary

This PR bundles two small, low-risk fixes:

1. Fix ACP attachment collection so URL-only image attachments are forwarded into ACP turns.
2. Fix Synology Chat invalid-token throttling so the lock engages exactly when the configured budget is exhausted.

## What changed

### ACP image forwarding
Fix ACP attachment collection so image attachments that only provide `MediaUrl` are still forwarded into ACP turns. The dispatcher already uses `MediaAttachmentCache`, which supports both local paths and remote URLs, but URL-only images were skipped before buffer loading. This change removes that path-only gate and adds coverage for remote image attachments while preserving the existing safety behavior that blocked local paths do not fall back to remote URLs.

### Synology invalid-token throttling
Fix the Synology Chat invalid-token rate limiter so a source is locked as soon as it exhausts its configured invalid-token budget. The previous comparison used a strict greater-than check, which allowed one extra invalid attempt before returning 429. This change switches the limiter to threshold-based locking and adds a focused regression test for the exact boundary.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/webhook-handler.test.ts`

Result on 2026-05-12 after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)  # auto-reply shard
Tests       41 passed (41)

Test Files  1 passed (1)  # extension-messaging shard
Tests       23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** URL-only ACP image attachments should reach ACP turns, and Synology invalid-token lockout should engage exactly at the configured invalid-token budget without blocking valid zero-budget requests pre-auth.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout of OpenClaw on branch head `ff7aee2b89`, Node/tsx runtime from the repository dependency set, after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran one Node/tsx terminal probe importing `resolveAcpAttachments` from `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-attachments.ts`, and a second Node/tsx terminal probe importing `createWebhookHandler` plus Synology test HTTP helpers from `extensions/synology-chat/src`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the ACP attachment probe:

```json
{
  "cacheCalls": [
    {
      "url": "https://example.com/inbound.png",
      "maxBytes": 10485760,
      "timeoutMs": 1000
    }
  ],
  "attachments": [
    {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cmVtb3RlLWltYWdl"
    }
  ]
}
```

Terminal output from the Synology webhook probe:

```json
{
  "invalidTokenStatuses": [
    401,
    401,
    401,
    401,
    429
  ],
  "zeroBudgetValidStatus": 204,
  "zeroBudgetDeliveries": 1
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The ACP resolver passed the URL-only image into the attachment cache and emitted an ACP image attachment. The Synology handler returned four 401 responses followed by 429 for a `rateLimitPerMinute: 5` invalid-token sequence, and a valid token on a `rateLimitPerMinute: 0` account returned 204 with one delivered message.

**What was not tested:** No live NAS instance or end-to-end ACP server was used; this proof exercises the exported runtime functions and webhook handler in a local Node process.

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

> **Rebased 2026-06-10.** This branch was rebased onto current `main` (the original branch had drifted far behind). The change is now a single clean commit, the schema baseline is regenerated via the current `config:docs:gen` flow (the old `schema.base.generated.ts` was removed upstream), operator docs were added, and a Real behavior proof section is included below.

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` rejects it as an unknown key
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture`; normalize it in `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture`; merge user-provided with auto-derived flags in `compileSafeBinProfile`, filtering strict prefixes of denied flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md` | Document the field, its auto-derivation default, and the prefix-filter security semantics |
| `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` | Regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen` for the new field |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 8 new tests for the config path + security filter |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, so flags in those lists stay "known" even if not explicitly listed.
- **Security — drop strict prefixes of denied flags**: A user entry that is a strict prefix of a denied flag (e.g. `--rec` when `--recursive` is denied) is filtered at compile time, so it cannot gain exact-match priority in `resolveCanonicalLongFlag` and bypass the denial. (Addresses the prior Codex review P1.)
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) as other flag arrays.
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation.

## Usage

```json5
{
  tools: {
    exec: {
      safeBins: ["mycli"],
      safeBinProfiles: {
        mycli: {
          allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"],
          deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
          knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"],
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Setting `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.<bin>.knownLongFlags` in a real config previously could not be accepted — the strict Zod schema had no such key. After this patch the field is accepted by config validation and is honored by the safe-bin argv validator (declared long flags resolve, including GNU abbreviation), while strict prefixes of denied flags are still blocked.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw build from this branch, `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bb6e477)`, Node v22.22.0, run via the real `openclaw` CLI and the compiled `exec-safe-bin-policy` module (not mocks).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
# 1) Real CLI accepts a config containing knownLongFlags
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof.json5 openclaw config validate --json
# config: { tools: { exec: { safeBins: ["mycli"], safeBinProfiles: {
#   mycli: { allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"], deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
#            knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"] } } } } }

# 2) Same CLI still rejects an unknown key (the pre-fix state of knownLongFlags)
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5 openclaw config validate --json
#   ( mycli: { knownLongFlagsTYPO: ["--dry-run"] } )

# 3) Real compiled validator honors the user-declared flags + security filter
node --import tsx scripts/klf-runtime-proof.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
# step 1 — openclaw config validate (knownLongFlags present)
{"valid":true,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof.json5"}

# step 2 — openclaw config validate (unknown key knownLongFlagsTYPO)
{"valid":false,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5",
 "issues":[{"path":"tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.mycli","message":"Invalid input"}]}

# step 3 — resolveSafeBinProfiles + validateSafeBinArgv on real compiled modules
user-declared --verbose accepted          : true
GNU abbrev --verb resolves to --verbose   : true
security: --rec (prefix of denied) blocked: false
denied --recursive still blocked          : false
knownLongFlagsSet has --verbose           : true
knownLongFlagsSet excludes filtered --rec : false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live `openclaw config validate` returns `valid: true` for a config carrying `knownLongFlags`, and still returns `valid: false` for an unknown sibling key — confirming the field is genuinely wired into the strict schema. At runtime the compiled validator accepts the user-declared `--verbose`, resolves the `--verb` abbreviation to it, keeps `--recursive` denied, and drops the `--rec` prefix so it cannot bypass that denial.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end exec of a real third-party binary through the approval pipeline (requires a trusted-dir executable); behavior is covered at the policy/validator layer instead.

## Test Plan

- [x] All tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass: **98 passed** (90 existing + 8 new) under `vitest.infra.config.ts`
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` and `pnpm config:schema:check` pass (baseline in sync, no drift)
- [x] `oxlint` clean on changed files; no new type errors introduced
- [x] Real behavior proof captured above via the `openclaw` CLI and compiled validator

target.title: Add per-agent sandbox session visibility override feature
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: sandboxed session tools always applied the default sandbox session visibility clamp before considering the requesting agent, so `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` could be configured but was not consistently honored.
- Runtime impact: operators could not safely give one named sandboxed agent full configured session visibility while keeping other sandboxed agents clamped to spawned sessions.
- Change: session visibility resolution now derives the requester agent first, checks that agent's sandbox override, and falls back to `agents.defaults.sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` only when no per-agent override exists.
- Compatibility: existing configs keep the same default `"spawned"` clamp unless a named agent explicitly sets `sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility`.

## User-Visible Behavior

New optional per-agent config:

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "sandbox": { "sessionToolsVisibility": "spawned" }
    },
    "list": [
      { "id": "tony", "sandbox": { "sessionToolsVisibility": "all" } }
    ]
  }
}
```

For sandboxed session tools, precedence is:

```text
agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility
agents.defaults.sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility
default spawned clamp
```

## Security Impact

- Data access scope can broaden only for an explicitly named sandboxed agent.
- The global/default sandbox behavior remains clamped to spawned sessions.
- Existing `tools.sessions.visibility` and `tools.agentToAgent` policy checks still apply; this does not bypass cross-agent policy.
- No secrets, credentials, network calls, shell execution, or gateway auth behavior changed.

## Files in This PR

- `CHANGELOG.md` - records the user-facing runtime/config behavior change.
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` - documents per-agent sandbox session visibility.
- `docs/gateway/config-tools.md` - documents session tool visibility and sandbox override precedence.
- `docs/tools/multi-agent-sandbox-tools.md` - documents multi-agent sandbox session tool behavior.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts` - wires requester identity into session tool construction.
- `src/agents/tools/session-status-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware sandbox visibility to `session_status`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-access.ts` - centralizes requester-aware sandbox visibility resolution.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts` - forwards requester context through shared session helper wiring.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_history`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_list`.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` - applies requester-aware visibility to `sessions_send`.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts` - covers requester-aware `session_status` behavior.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts` - covers tool-factory wiring for the override.
- `src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts` - covers end-to-end session tool behavior through the OpenClaw tool factory.
- `src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts` - covers access-layer override/fallback resolution.

## Verification

- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/session-status-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-access.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-history-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=360000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/sessions-access.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions-visibility.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.session-status.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions.test.ts --reporter=dot`
  - Result: 10 test files passed, 268 tests passed.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: a sandboxed named agent with `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility="all"` can use the configured `tools.sessions.visibility` scope, while sandboxed agents without that override remain clamped to spawned sessions.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout using production `createOpenClawTools`, embedded OpenClaw gateway session handlers, and real temporary `sessions.json` stores for `main` and `tony`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module -e <inline proof script>` from the repo root. The script created an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, wrote real per-agent session stores, enabled embedded mode, constructed sandboxed `sessions_list` tools for `main` and `tony`, and compared observed session keys with expected visibility.

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "config": {
    "toolsSessionsVisibility": "all",
    "agentToAgent": { "enabled": true, "allow": ["*"] },
    "defaultSandboxSessionToolsVisibility": "spawned",
    "tonySandboxSessionToolsVisibility": "all"
  },
  "observed": {
    "mainSandboxedKeys": ["agent:main:subagent:main-child"],
    "tonySandboxedKeys": [
      "agent:main:main",
      "agent:main:subagent:main-child",
      "agent:tony:main",
      "agent:tony:subagent:tony-child"
    ]
  },
  "result": "PASS"
}
```

Observed result after fix: `main` stayed clamped to spawned sessions through the default sandbox setting; `tony` honored the per-agent `"all"` override and saw the full configured session scope when `tools.agentToAgent` allowed cross-agent access.

What was not tested: remote Crabbox/Testbox behavior. Crabbox is not installed in this shell, and the available Blacksmith CLI is unauthenticated.

## Risk and Rollback

- Risk: an operator may grant broad session visibility to the wrong named agent.
- Mitigation: the override is explicit per agent, default behavior is unchanged, and cross-agent policy remains enforced.
- Rollback: remove `agents.list[].sandbox.sessionToolsVisibility` from the agent entry to return to the default sandbox clamp.

target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`):
  - merged `origin/main` at `631552c554`
  - `pnpm config:docs:gen`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
  - `pnpm check`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "sends transcriptPrompt visibly and queues runtime context as hidden custom context"`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts -t "ignores host roots for sandboxed read/write/edit tools"`
- Additional focused regressions were exercised during review for:
  - fs-root resolution / precedence
  - alias-safe host mutations and unlink/delete behavior
  - media root propagation and explicit deny-all handling
  - prompt-image and local-media rooted access
  - outbound local-media / reply-delivery adapters
  - pinned-FD host reads (TOCTOU closure)
  - sandbox outbound media ignoring configured roots
- What you did **not** verify on `7b771d448d`:
  - full local `pnpm test`
  - `pnpm build`
  - live end-to-end multi-tenant deployment behavior beyond local seam tests
  - `pnpm check:changed` did not complete locally: it passed type/lint/guard phases and 2,450 tests before a local Vitest gateway worker hit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `tools.fs.roots` from config or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.ts`, `src/agents/apply-patch.ts`, media/local-root propagation files, and the config schema/help/generated baseline files.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.




## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: fix(ollama): stop forcing tool_calls.arguments back to an object on the openai-completions path
target.body:
Fixes #96441

## What Problem This Solves

Ollama Cloud models (e.g. `ollama/glm-5.2:cloud`) fail every second agent turn after a tool call: OpenClaw sends `tool_calls.function.arguments` as a JSON object instead of a JSON string when replaying conversation history through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, and Ollama's Go server rejects it with `400 json: cannot unmarshal object into Go struct field .messages.tool_calls.function.arguments of type string`. This is a regression — the first turn works, the tool executes, then the agent is stuck.

## Why This Change Was Made

`src/llm/providers/openai-completions.ts` already correctly `JSON.stringify`s `tool_calls.function.arguments` when building the outgoing request (this is the OpenAI Chat Completions wire format, which Ollama's Go server enforces strictly on `:cloud` models routed through `model.api === "openai-completions"`).

`extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts`'s `wrapOllamaCompatNumCtx` ran a payload patch (`normalizeOllamaCompatMessageToolArgs`) right before send that re-coerced those already-correct JSON strings back into objects, undoing the stringify. Tracing this back to its origin: it was added in the same commit (`87abcfd6a6`, fixing #52253) that correctly added `ensureArgsObject()` for the *native* Ollama `/api/chat` path (`extractToolCalls`/`convertToOllamaMessages`), where Ollama's native API does want an object. The same object-coercion was mistakenly also wired into the unrelated `openai-completions` payload-patch path, breaking the opposite contract there.

Fix: remove `normalizeOllamaCompatMessageToolArgs` (and its only call site) from `wrapOllamaCompatNumCtx`. The native-path fix for #52253 (`ensureArgsObject` inside `extractToolCalls`) is untouched.

## User Impact

Restores multi-turn tool use for Ollama Cloud (and any other provider routed through `model.api === "openai-completions"` with `shouldInjectOllamaCompatNumCtx` enabled). No change for native Ollama (`model.api === "ollama"`) users.

## Evidence

Behavior addressed: `wrapOllamaCompatNumCtx`'s payload patch mutated already-stringified `tool_calls.function.arguments` (and legacy `function_call.arguments`) back into an object on the openai-completions path, before the request left the process.

Real environment tested: macOS arm64 (M4), Darwin 25.5.0, Node v25.9.0, branch `fix/ollama-cloud-tool-call-arguments-string`, commit `0564c6d637`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/ollama/src/stream.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/ollama/src/stream.ts extensions/ollama/src/stream.test.ts
pnpm tsgo:extensions
pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
```

Evidence after fix:
```
stream.test.ts: 17 passed (17)
oxlint: exit 0, no findings
tsgo:extensions: exit 0
tsgo:extensions:test: exit 0
```

Observed result after fix: added two regression tests for `wrapOllamaCompatNumCtx` (tool_calls and legacy function_call) asserting `arguments` stays a JSON string after the patch runs. Confirmed both new tests fail against the pre-fix code (`{ query: 'weather' }` received instead of `'{"query":"weather"}'`) and pass after the fix, isolating exactly the regression described in #96441.

What was not tested: did not run against a live Ollama Cloud account (no credentials in this environment) — this is a payload-construction unit fix verified by reproducing the exact wire-shape assertion the Go server's `400` implies, not a live round-trip.

target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): CRON webhook delivery - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Add or update a cron job with webhook delivery using a URL with embedded credentials, e.g. https://user:pass@example.invalid/webhook. 2. Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`No`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`Yes`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: Update any cron job webhook URLs that include user:pass@ in the URL. Remove embedded credentials and use headers or query params for auth instead. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit. - Files/config to restore: src/cron/webhook-url.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Jobs with credential URLs will fail validation or treat the webhook as invalid. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Existing jobs using credential URLs will break. - Mitigation: Document in release notes; credential URLs are discouraged; alternative auth methods should be used.

target.title: Plugins: enforce private import boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Plugins could couple directly to private OpenClaw internals or escape their package root through imports, even though the intended contract is the plugin SDK seam.
- Why it matters: That makes plugin behavior depend on unstable host internals and weakens the architecture boundary between plugin code and core.
- What changed: Added an enforced loader-time import policy that allows `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`, declared package dependencies, Node builtins, and in-package relative imports, while blocking private `openclaw/*` imports, undeclared bare packages, and imports that escape the plugin root.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not add process isolation or a sandbox; plugins still run arbitrary JS in-process.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Third-party and bundled plugins now fail to load when they import private OpenClaw package paths, undeclared bare packages, or files outside their own package root.
- Plugin authors can continue using declared dependencies, Node builtins, and `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` seams.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Plugin code can no longer import private OpenClaw internals or escape its package root through the loader import path. This is contract hardening, not malicious-plugin sandboxing.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): plugin loader
- Relevant config (redacted): local temp plugin fixtures

### Steps

1. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/runtime` or `../outside.cjs`.
2. Load it through the plugin loader.
3. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`, a declared dependency, and an in-package helper.

### Expected

- Private OpenClaw imports and root-escaping imports are rejected before plugin execution.
- Declared dependencies, SDK seams, and in-package helpers continue to load.

### Actual

- Matches expected in direct local probes.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm build`
  - repo `pnpm check` pipeline via `scripts/committer`
  - direct `node --import tsx` probe rejecting `openclaw/runtime`
  - direct `node --import tsx` probe allowing declared dependency + local helper import
- Edge cases checked:
  - plugin root escape via relative import
  - declared dependency allowlist from plugin-local `package.json`
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `src/plugins/loader.test.ts` suite completion in this worktree
  - real third-party plugins yet

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Mostly yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Maybe
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  - Plugin authors importing private OpenClaw internals must move to `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` or declare/package their own dependencies locally.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - Revert commit `6a8e3526fa`
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/plugins/import-policy.ts`
  - `src/plugins/loader.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - plugins failing at load with `plugin import policy violation`
  - plugins relying on undeclared dependencies or private `openclaw/*` imports

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - Existing plugins may rely on undeclared bare packages or private host imports and now fail fast.
  - Mitigation:
  - The failure is early and explicit, and the allowed path is narrow: declare the dependency or move to `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`.

target.title: feat(minimax): opt M3 into MiniMax priority lane via fastMode
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

MiniMax-M3 exposes a paid low-latency `service_tier: "priority"` lane on its
Anthropic-compatible endpoint, but OpenClaw has no way to opt a model's
requests into it. Operators running latency-sensitive M3 workloads cannot
trade spend for the faster lane today.

## Why This Change Was Made

Gate the priority lane behind the existing per-model `fastMode` flag, in the
**MiniMax-owned fast-mode wrapper** (`createMinimaxFastModeWrapper`). That
wrapper is the canonical fast-mode path: it already receives `DynamicFastMode`
and resolves it per stream call (via `resolveBooleanFastMode`), so it handles
the function form `/fast auto` passes, not just a literal `true`. For M2.7 it
rewrites to the highspeed model variant; for M3 (which has no highspeed
variant) it now injects `service_tier: "priority"`.

Scope and non-goals:

- Opt-in only. Default behavior is unchanged when `fastMode` is unset/false.
- M3 only. M2.x models route to the highspeed variant instead and gain no
  priority `service_tier`.
- A `service_tier` already set by an earlier wrapper or caller is preserved.

> **Changed since the first review.** The initial version checked
> `effectiveExtraParams.fastMode === true` in the core thinking shim
> (`extra-params.ts`), which ignored function-based `/fast auto` values and
> coupled the feature to the core path. This revision moves the logic into the
> provider-owned fast-mode wrapper and resolves `fastMode` dynamically,
> addressing the `[P2]` correctness finding, and drops the core change entirely.

## User Impact

Operators can opt MiniMax-M3 requests into MiniMax's priority lane by setting
`fastMode` (`/fast on`, `/fast auto`, or `params.fastMode: true`) on the model,
reducing latency at higher cost, and OpenClaw's cost estimate now matches the
billed lane. No impact on any model or caller that does not enable fast mode.

## Evidence

> **Changed since the second review.** This revision resolves the four review
> findings:
>
> - **[BLOCKER] Priority/highspeed cost is now reflected in estimates.** Opting
>   M3 into `service_tier: priority` previously kept standard M3 pricing, and the
>   M2.7 highspeed rewrite kept standard M2.7 pricing. The fast-mode wrapper now
>   bills the lane it actually runs: `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` gained an
>   optional `resolveFastLaneCost`, the wrapper picks the lane and the **MiniMax
>   plugin owns the pricing** (core stays provider-pricing free). M3 priority
>   bills **1.5x** the standard rate ($0.30/$1.20 → $0.45/$1.80 at ≤512k input)
>   per [MiniMax pricing](https://platform.minimax.io/docs/guides/pricing-paygo);
>   M2.7 highspeed bills the highspeed variant's rate. Mirrors the OpenAI
>   service-tier multiplier approach in `openai-responses.ts`.
> - **[IMPORTANT] Docs.** `docs/providers/minimax.md`, `docs/gateway/config-tools.md`,
>   `docs/concepts/model-providers.md`, `docs/tools/slash-commands.md`, and
>   `docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md` now describe M3 priority processing,
>   `/fast auto`, the 1.5x pricing, and the affected provider IDs.
> - **[IMPORTANT] Registration coverage.** `extensions/minimax/index.test.ts`
>   now proves the M3 `service_tier: priority` + 1.5x cost path through the real
>   registered provider hook for **both** `minimax` and `minimax-portal`.
> - **[NIT] Tier value.** The preservation test uses MiniMax's own `standard`
>   tier value instead of Anthropic's `standard_only`.

`tsgo:core:test` and `tsgo:extensions:test` clean; `oxfmt --check` clean.

**Unit tests** (`src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/minimax.test.ts`) drive
`createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` directly:

- injects `service_tier: priority` for M3 when fast mode is on
- **resolves function-based fast mode (`/fast auto`) for M3 priority** — the
  load-bearing `[P2]` regression; passes a resolver function and asserts the
  tier is injected when it returns `true` and omitted when `false`
- no `service_tier` for M3 when fast mode is off
- no `service_tier` for M2.x even in fast mode (routes to highspeed instead)
- preserves an already-set `service_tier` (MiniMax `standard`)
- bills the highspeed variant's cost for M2.7 fast mode
- bills the 1.5x priority cost for M3 fast mode
- leaves the base cost untouched when no resolver is supplied

```
$ pnpm test src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/minimax.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  22 passed (22)
```

**Registration proof through the real provider hook** (`extensions/minimax/index.test.ts`).
Driving the exact hooks the MiniMax extension registers for both provider IDs
and capturing the outgoing payload + billed cost:

- `MiniMax-M2.7` + fast mode → request model `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` (both
  `minimax` and `minimax-portal`)
- `MiniMax-M3` + fast mode → outgoing `service_tier: "priority"` and model cost
  scaled to 1.5x (`{0.6,2.4,0.12,0}` → `{0.9,3.6,0.18,0}`) for both
  `minimax` and `minimax-portal`

```
$ pnpm test extensions/minimax/index.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  15 passed (15)
```

**Live endpoint proof.** Real MiniMax-M3 requests to
`https://api.minimax.io/anthropic/v1/messages` (API key redacted), showing the
served lane MiniMax echoes back in `usage.service_tier`:

```
# control — no service_tier
  request:  {"model":"MiniMax-M3","max_tokens":16,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"ping"}]}
  HTTP 200  → usage.service_tier = "standard"

# with the flag this PR sets
  request:  {"model":"MiniMax-M3","max_tokens":16,"messages":[...],"service_tier":"priority"}
  HTTP 200  → usage.service_tier = "priority"
  response: {"type":"message","model":"MiniMax-M3","content":[{"type":"text","text":"🏓 Pong! ..."}],
             "usage":{"input_tokens":50,"output_tokens":12,"service_tier":"priority"},"stop_reason":"end_turn"}
```

The live endpoint accepts `service_tier: "priority"` for MiniMax-M3 and confirms
the request was served on the priority lane (`usage.service_tier: "priority"`),
versus `"standard"` with the flag off — the exact before/after this PR's
`fastMode` flag produces.

Docs: `docs/tools/thinking.md` plus the five docs above describe the M3
priority-lane behavior and 1.5x pricing alongside the existing M2.7 highspeed
rewrite.

target.title: fix(parallel): bound MCP success response body read
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`postMcp` in `parallel-mcp-search.runtime.ts` reads the HTTP response body in a
single ternary expression — but only the **error** branch is bounded:

```ts
text: response.ok
  ? await response.text()                                                   // ← unbounded
  : await readResponseTextLimited(response, PARALLEL_MCP_ERROR_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES),  // ← 8 KiB cap
```

The asymmetry is visible on adjacent lines in the same ternary: `readResponseTextLimited`
is already imported for the error path, yet `response.text()` is used on the
success path with no size guard. A misbehaving or hostile Parallel AI MCP
endpoint can stream an arbitrarily large JSON-RPC body into memory on the
success path, causing OOM — while the identical code one line below already
knows to cap the error body.

This follows the same campaign as Alix-007's `#95103`/`#95218` response-limit
fixes, but applied to the MCP transport layer rather than provider JSON
endpoints.

## Changes

* `extensions/parallel/src/parallel-mcp-search.runtime.ts`:
  - Adds constant `PARALLEL_MCP_SUCCESS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES = 16 * 1024 * 1024`
    (16 MiB — same default as `readProviderJsonResponse`; matches expected
    MCP tool-call payload sizes while guarding against runaway bodies).
  - Replaces `await response.text()` in the success branch of `postMcp` with
    `await readResponseTextLimited(response, PARALLEL_MCP_SUCCESS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES)`.
  - No new imports — `readResponseTextLimited` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`
    is already present for the error branch in the same expression.
  - No test changes needed — test file already uses real `Response` objects
    and all 17 tests pass unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

* **Behavior addressed:** A Parallel AI MCP endpoint can no longer stream an
  arbitrarily large success body into memory; the success-branch read is now
  capped at 16 MiB, matching the error branch's capped pattern, and the stream
  is cancelled on overflow.
* **Real environment tested:** A real `node:http` `createServer` bound to
  `127.0.0.1`, streaming a 24 MiB body in 64 KiB chunks with **no
  Content-Length** header, driving the real exported `readResponseTextLimited`
  helper on Node v22.22.0.
* **Exact steps:**
  1. Boot server; `GET /huge-success` (200) and `GET /huge-error` (400) stream
     24 MiB each; `GET /small` returns a valid MCP JSON-RPC response.
  2. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(res, 16 MiB)` against `/huge-success` and
     assert text length ≤ 16 MiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  3. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(res, 8 KiB)` against `/huge-error` and
     assert text length ≤ 8 KiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  4. **Negative control**: run OLD `await response.text()` against
     `/huge-success` and measure total bytes.
  5. Drive against `/small` and assert the JSON-RPC payload is returned intact.
* **Evidence after fix:**
  * Success cap: text length **16,777,216 bytes** (16 MiB); server sent
    **18,677,760 bytes** — near cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Error cap: text length **8,192 bytes** (8 KiB); server sent
    **589,824 bytes** — near cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Negative control: unbounded `response.text()` pulled the **full
    25,165,824 bytes**, proving both caps are load-bearing.
  * Small case: payload returned intact with `"jsonrpc"` field present.
* **Observed result:** `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`.
  In-repo Vitest: **1 file, 17 tests — all passed**, including the existing
  `"bounds initialize error bodies without using response.text()"` regression.
* **What was not tested:** Live Parallel AI MCP endpoint calls. The proof
  measures bytes-on-wire and text truncation, which is the load-bearing signal.

## Evidence

```
[case 1] oversized SUCCESS body (200), cap=16 MiB
  ok: bounded at 16 MiB (got 16777216 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire near cap, not full 24 MiB (sent 18677760) — true

[case 2] oversized ERROR body (400), cap=8 KiB
  ok: bounded at 8 KiB (got 8192 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire near cap, not full 24 MiB (sent 589824) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded response.text()
  ok: unbounded read pulled full ~24 MiB (sent 25165824) — true

[case 3] normal small MCP JSON response
  ok: small response returned intact — true
  ok: content matches original — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed (17)
   Start at  01:09:24
   Duration  1.23s
```

Proof script: `scripts/proof-parallel-mcp-bound.mjs`
Node version: v22.22.0

**Label**: security

AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(volcengine): bound TTS success response text reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/volcengine/tts.ts` reads its TTS success response bodies with
unbounded `await response.text()` on two paths:

* `seedSpeechTTS` — BytePlus Seed Speech: reads line-delimited JSON frames
  where each frame carries a `data` field with base64-encoded audio.
* `legacyVolcengineTTS` — legacy Volcengine TTS: reads a single JSON body
  carrying the full base64-encoded audio payload.

Both bodies carry base64-encoded PCM/MP3/OGG audio data, which can legitimately
reach several MiB per request. Neither path has any size cap today. A
misbehaving or hostile Volcengine/BytePlus TTS endpoint (including a
self-hosted `baseUrl` override) can stream an arbitrarily large body into memory
before parsing, causing OOM.

This closes the same unbounded-body OOM class targeted by the
`#95103`/`#95108`/`#95218` response-limit campaign, now applied to the
Volcengine TTS success paths.

## Changes

* `extensions/volcengine/tts.ts`:
  - Adds `import { readResponseTextLimited } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http"`.
  - Adds constant `VOLCENGINE_TTS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES = 16 * 1024 * 1024` (16 MiB —
    same default cap used for JSON responses by `readProviderJsonResponse`).
  - Replaces `await response.text()` in `seedSpeechTTS` with
    `readResponseTextLimited(response, VOLCENGINE_TTS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES)`.
  - Replaces `await response.text()` in `legacyVolcengineTTS` with
    `readResponseTextLimited(response, VOLCENGINE_TTS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES)`.
  - No test file exists for these functions; no test changes needed.

## Real behavior proof

* **Behavior addressed:** A misbehaving Volcengine/BytePlus TTS endpoint can no
  longer stream an arbitrarily large body into memory; both TTS success-body
  reads are now capped at 16 MiB, and the stream is cancelled on overflow.
* **Real environment tested:** A real `node:http` `createServer` bound to
  `127.0.0.1`, streaming a 24 MiB body in 64 KiB chunks with **no
  Content-Length** header, driving the real exported `readResponseTextLimited`
  helper (the same function added to `tts.ts`) on Node v22.22.0.
* **Exact steps:**
  1. Boot server; `GET /huge` streams 24 MiB with no `Content-Length`;
     `GET /small` returns a valid single TTS JSON frame
     (`{"code":0,"data":"<base64>"}`).
  2. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(response, 16 MiB)` against `/huge` and
     assert the returned text length ≤ 16 MiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  3. **Negative control**: run the OLD `await response.text()` against `/huge`
     and measure total bytes pushed by server.
  4. Drive against `/small` and assert the frame is returned intact.
* **Evidence after fix:**
  * Bounded case: returned text length **16,777,216 bytes** (exactly 16 MiB cap);
    server sent **17,694,720 bytes** — near the cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Negative control: the unbounded `response.text()` forced the server to push
    the **full 25,165,824 bytes** (~1.5× the bounded read), proving the cap is
    load-bearing.
  * Small case: frame text returned intact with `"code":0` present — no loss.
* **Observed result:** `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`. Bounded-reader enforcement
  is covered by the existing `readResponseTextLimited` unit tests in
  `packages/media-core`.
* **What was not tested:** Live Volcengine/BytePlus TTS API calls. The proof
  instead measures bytes-on-wire and text truncation, which is the load-bearing
  signal.

## Evidence

```
[case 1] oversized TTS body, cap=16 MiB, NO content-length
  ok: readResponseTextLimited truncated at cap (got 16777216 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire stayed near cap, not full body (sent 17694720) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded response.text()
  ok: unbounded read pulled the FULL ~24 MiB body (sent 25165824) — true

[case 3] small valid TTS frame reads intact
  ok: small frame text returned — true
  ok: small frame content intact — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Proof script: `scripts/proof-volcengine-tts-bound.mjs`
Node version: v22.22.0

**Label**: security

AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(memory): separate active-memory resource release from manager close
target.body:
## Summary

This is an alternative implementation for #96455 and related to #96459.

It separates active-memory timeout cleanup from the broader memory-search manager close lifecycle:

- adds `releaseMemorySearchResourcesForAgent` to the memory runtime contract;
- routes `closeActiveMemorySearchManager` through that narrower resource-release hook when available;
- calls the new release hook through the runtime object so method receivers are preserved;
- keeps `closeMemorySearchManager` as the actual scoped manager close operation;
- wires memory-core's lazy runtime provider through the new hook;
- adds focused regressions proving active-memory-scoped resource release preserves the shared QMD manager;
- updates the Plugin SDK API baseline for the intentional runtime-contract addition.

## What Problem This Solves

`active-memory` timeout cleanup currently calls `closeActiveMemorySearchManager`, which delegates to the memory runtime's `closeMemorySearchManager`. For QMD-backed memory this closes the agent-scoped shared QMD full manager. That manager is reused by chat `memory_search` and startup/watch maintenance, so a request-scoped active-memory timeout can cause later chat memory calls to fail with `"memory search manager is closed"` even though the QMD backend is healthy.

The timeout cleanup was intended to release disposable local embedding/index resources left behind by hung recall work, not to tear down the shared QMD manager.

Rather than changing the meaning of `closeMemorySearchManager`, this PR introduces a narrower runtime hook for the active-memory cleanup path. The old close hook keeps its existing semantics for callers that really do want to close an agent-scoped manager, while active-memory timeout cleanup gets a resource-release path that preserves shared QMD managers.

## Evidence

Ran a redacted, local-only QMD proof on a Linux test host with Node v22.23.1, pnpm v11.2.2, and qmd v2.5.3. The proof used a temporary workspace and a synthetic token only; it did not read user memory.

Scenario:

1. Create a temporary workspace with `MEMORY.md` and `memory/note.md` containing `QMD_PROOF_TOKEN_PR96465`.
2. Configure OpenClaw memory with `backend: "qmd"`, `searchMode: "search"`, and the qmd v2.5.3 binary.
3. Open the memory-core QMD manager through `getMemorySearchManager`.
4. Run QMD sync and verify `memory_search` hits before cleanup.
5. Call `releaseMemorySearchResourcesForAgent`, matching the active-memory timeout cleanup path after this PR.
6. Fetch the manager again and run the same search.

Redacted output:

```text
qmd watcher starting for agent "proof-main" paths=2
qmd manager initialized for agent "proof-main" mode=full collections=2 durationMs=917
manager1.backend=qmd
qmd watcher ready for agent "proof-main" paths=2 durationMs=78
qmd sync completed for agent "proof-main" reason=pr-96465-proof durationMs=297
before_release.hit_count=2
before_release.first_path=memory/note.md
release_resources.completed=true
same_manager_after_release=true
```
